title: Two-way tables, Venn diagrams, and probability
description: Practice using the addition rule in two-way tables and Venn diagrams to calculate probabilities.
topic_path: Get ready for probability and combinatorics
Two-way tables, Venn diagrams, and probability

assessment_items.question_content:
Fifty people were surveyed about their preference between chocolate and vanilla cake. The following two-way table displays data for the  sample of people who responded to the survey.

Preference | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Prefers chocolate | $18$ | $15$ | $33$
Prefers vanilla | $6$ | $5$ | $11$
No preference | $4$ | $2$ | $6$
**TOTAL** | $28$ | $22$ | $50$ 

**In this sample, are the events "female" and "prefers chocolate" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample is female OR prefers chocolate.**

$P\left(\text{female OR prefers chocolate}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who are female and prefer chocolate.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a female OR someone who prefers chocolate

There were $\blueD{22}$ females, and $\purpleD{33}$ people who preferred chocolate. We can't just add these together because we would be double counting the $\maroonD{15}$ people who were both female and preferred chocolate.


Preference | Male | Female | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: |
Prefers chocolate | $18$ | $\maroonD{15}$ | $\purpleD{33}$
Prefers vanilla | $6$ | $5$ | $11$
No preference | $4$ | $2$ | $6$
**TOTAL** | $28$ | $\blueD{22}$ | $50$

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{female OR prefers chocolate}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{female}\right)+P\left(\text{prefers chocolate}\right)-P\left(\text{both}\right)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{22}}{50}+\dfrac{\purpleD{33}}{50}-\dfrac{\maroonD{15}}{50}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{40}{50}
\\\\
&=0.8
\end{align}$

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{female OR prefers chocolate}\right)=0.8$

assessment_items.question_content:
The principal of a high school needs to plan seating for commencement. The following two-way table displays data for the students who are involved in this year's commencement ceremony.

Grade level| In choir | Not in choir| TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Junior | $15$ | $3$ | $18$
Senior | $23$ | $436$ | $459$
Other | $3$ | $0$ | $3$
**TOTAL** | $41$ | $439$ | $480$

**For these students, are the events "senior" and "not in choir" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected student from this group is a senior OR is not in choir.**

$P\left(\text{senior OR not in choir}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who are seniors and are not in choir.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a senior OR someone who is not in choir

There were $\blueD{459}$ seniors, and $\purpleD{439}$ students who are not in choir. We can't just add these together because we would be double counting the $\maroonD{436}$ students who were seniors but not in choir.


Grade level| In choir | Not in choir| TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Junior | $15$ | $3$ | $18$
Senior | $23$ | $\maroonD{436}$ | $\blueD{459}$
Other | $3$ | $0$ | $3$
**TOTAL** | $41$ | $\purpleD{439}$ | $480$

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{senior OR not in choir}\right)=0.9625$

assessment_items.question_content:
Two hundred people were surveyed about whether or not they like recreational reading and academic reading. The following two-way table displays data for the  sample of people who responded to the survey.

Opinion| Liked academic reading| Disliked academic reading| TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Liked recreational reading | $136$ | $40$ | $176$
Disliked recreational reading  | $16$ | $8$ | $24$
**TOTAL** | $152$ | $48$ | $200$ 

**In this sample, are the events "liked recreational reading" and "disliked recreational reading" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample liked recreational reading OR disliked recreational reading.**

$P\left(\text{liked recreational OR disliked recreational}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

The participants in the sample could not both like and dislike recreational reading.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a person who liked recreational reading OR a person who disliked recreational reading

There were $\blueD{176}$ people who liked recreational reading and $\purpleD{24}$ people who disliked recreational reading. Since these events are mutually exclusive, we can just add these together.


Opinion| Liked academic reading| Disliked academic reading| TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Liked recreational reading | $136$ | $40$ | $\blueD{176}$
Disliked recreational reading  | $16$ | $8$ | $\purpleD{24}$
**TOTAL** | $152$ | $48$ | $200$

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{liked recreational OR disliked recreational}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{liked recreational}\right)+P\left(\text{disliked recreational}\right)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{176}}{200}+\dfrac{\purpleD{24}}{200}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{200}{200}
\\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{liked recreational OR disliked recreational}\right)=1$

assessment_items.question_content:
A hospital tracked the day of the week each baby was born and whether or not the delivery was scheduled in advance. The following two-way table displays data for the  sample of babies born in a particular year at that hospital.

Day of birth| Scheduled | Unscheduled | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Sunday | $9$ | $31$ | $40$
Monday | $19$ | $66$ | $85$
Tuesday | $20$ | $70$ | $90$
Wednesday | $17$ | $61$ | $78$
Thursday | $19$ | $68$ | $87$
Friday | $15$ | $55$ | $70$
Saturday | $11$ | $39$ | $50$
**TOTAL** | $110$ | $390$ | $500$

**In this sample, are the events "Tuesday" and "Friday" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected baby from this sample was born on Tuesday OR on Friday.**

$P\left(\text{Tuesday OR Friday}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

The participants in the sample could not be born both on Tuesday and on Friday.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a baby born on Tuesday OR a baby born on Friday

There were $\blueD{90}$ babies born on a Tuesday and $\purpleD{70}$ babies born on a Friday. Since these events are mutually exclusive, we can just add these together.


Day of birth| Scheduled | Unscheduled | TOTAL
- | :-: |:-: |:-: 
Sunday | $9$ | $31$ | $40$
Monday | $19$ | $66$ | $85$
Tuesday | $20$ | $70$ | $\blueD{90}$
Wednesday | $17$ | $61$ | $78$
Thursday | $19$ | $68$ | $87$
Friday | $15$ | $55$ | $\purpleD{70}$
Saturday | $11$ | $39$ | $50$
**TOTAL** | $110$ | $390$ | $500$

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{Tuesday OR Friday}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{Tuesday}\right)+P\left(\text{Friday}\right)
\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{90}}{500}+\dfrac{\purpleD{70}}{500}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{160}{500}
\\\\
&=0.32
\end{align}$

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{Tuesday OR Friday}\right)=0.32$

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant noted what type of food its customers purchased last week. Here are the results:

![A Venn Diagram has 2 overlapping groups: Burger, 15% and Fries, 10%. The overlapping area shared by both Burger and Fries is 55%. The area not included in any group is 20%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e9fa625583ed1ee2ab275ab2c5d246363e7461ad)

**In this sample, are the events "burger" and "fries" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample bought a burger OR bought fries.**

$P\left(\text{burger OR fries}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who bought both a burger and fries.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting someone who bought a burger OR someone who bought fries

There were $\purpleD{15\%}+55\%+\blueD{10\%}=80\%$ of the customers who bought a burger or fries.

![A Venn Diagram has 2 overlapping groups: Burger, 15% and Fries, 10%. The overlapping area shared by both Burger and Fries is 55%. The area not included in any group is 20%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e9fa625583ed1ee2ab275ab2c5d246363e7461ad)

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{burger OR fries}\right)=0.8$

assessment_items.question_content:
A salon owner noted what types of services its clients requested last week. Here are the results:

![A Venn Diagram has 3 overlapping groups: Dye, 5%; Haircut, 35%; and Permanent, 5%. The overlapping area shared by both Dye and Haircut is 30%. The overlapping area shared by both Haircut and Permanent is 10%. The area not included in any group is identified as 15%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/316cbf514f64eb8f7175f80a06dd14a41183bf2d)

**In this sample, are the events "dye" and "permanent" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample requested a dye OR a permanent.**

$P\left(\text{dye OR permanent}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are no people in this sample who requested both a dye and a permanent.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting someone who requested a dye OR someone who requested a permanent

There were $\greenD{5\%}+30\%+10\%+\blueD{5\%}=50\%$ of the clients who requested a dye or a permanent.

![A Venn Diagram has 3 overlapping groups: Dye, 5%; Haircut, 35%; and Permanent, 5%. The overlapping area shared by both Dye and Haircut is 30%. The overlapping area shared by both Haircut and Permanent is 10%. The area not included in any group is 15%.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/316cbf514f64eb8f7175f80a06dd14a41183bf2d)

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{dye OR permanent}\right)=0.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
The usher at a wedding asked each of the $80$ guests whether they were a friend of the bride or of the groom. Here are the results:

![A Venn Diagram has 2 overlapping groups: Bride, 17 and Groom, 20. The overlapping area shared by both Bride and Groom is 42. The area not included by any group is identified as 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fa1c9ea596c25ddc6cde32c134c04c54b9f92e49)

**In this sample, are the events "bride" and "groom" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample was a friend of the bride OR of the groom.**

$P\left(\text{bride OR groom}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who were friends of both the bride and the groom.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting someone who is a friend of the bride OR someone who is a friend of the groom

There were $\purpleD{17}+42+\blueD{20}=79$ of the $80$ guests who were friends with the bride OR the groom.

![A Venn Diagram has 2 overlapping groups: Bride, 17 and Groom, 20. The overlapping area shared by both Bride and Groom is 42. The area not included by any group is identified as 1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fa1c9ea596c25ddc6cde32c134c04c54b9f92e49)

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{bride OR groom}\right)=\dfrac{79}{80}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A business owner noted the features of the $100$ cars parked at the business. Here are the results:

![A Venn Diagram has 3 overlapping groups: Sunroof, 13; 2 Doors, 14; and 4 Doors, 25. The overlapping area shared by both 2 Doors and Sunroof is 18. The overlapping area shared by both 4 Doors and Sunroof is 20. The overlapping area shared by both 2 Doors and 4 Doors is 0.The area shared by all 3 groups is 0. The area not included in any group is identified as 10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8692c3d0452f644046dbd24020e6eb2fb315b0ba)

**In this sample, are the events "$2$ doors" and "$4$ doors" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected car from this sample had $2$ doors or $4$ doors.**

$P\left(\text{2 doors OR 4 doors}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are no cars in this sample that had both the two doors feature and four doors feature.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a car with $2$ doors OR a car with $4$ doors

There were $\purpleD{14}+18+20+\blueD{25}+0+0=77$ of the $100$ cars that had two doors or four doors.

![A Venn Diagram has 3 overlapping groups: Sunroof, 13; 2 Doors, 14; and 4 Doors, 25. The overlapping area shared by both 2 Doors and Sunroof is 18. The overlapping area shared by both 4 Doors and Sunroof is 20. The overlapping area shared by both 2 Doors and 4 Doors is 0.The area shared by all 3 groups is 0. The area not included in any group is identified as 10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8692c3d0452f644046dbd24020e6eb2fb315b0ba)

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{2 doors OR 4 doors}\right)=0.77$

assessment_items.question_content:
Employees at an office were surveyed about what types of hot beverages they drank that day. It was found that $50\%$ of them drank coffee and $25\%$ drank tea. The survey showed that $10\%$ of the employees drank both coffee and tea.

**For these employees, are the events "drank coffee" and "drank tea" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this group drank coffee OR drank tea.**

$P\left(\text{coffee OR tea}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who drank coffee and drank tea.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a person who drank coffee OR a person who drank tea

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{drank coffee OR drank tea}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{coffee}\right)+P\left(\text{prefers tea}\right)-P\left(\text{both}\right)
\\\\
&=0.50+0.25-0.10
\\\\
&=0.65
\end{align}$

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{drank coffee OR drank tea}\right)=0.65$

assessment_items.question_content:
The theater director offered every member of the drama one vote for which play they preferred to perform.  The director found that $35\%$ voted for *The Oddems Family*, that $57\%$ voted for *Thirteenth Night*, and that $8\%$ did not vote.

**In this group, are the events "*Oddems Family*" and "*Thirteenth Night*" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this group voted for *Oddems Family* OR *Thirteenth Night*.**

$P\left(\text{Oddems Family OR Thirteenth Night}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

Each person only got one vote, so they could not vote for both plays.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a person who voted for *Oddems Family* or a person who voted for *Thirteenth Night*

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{Oddems Family OR Thirteenth Night}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{Oddems Family}\right)+P\left(\text{Thirteenth Night}\right)-P\left(\text{both}\right)
\\\\
&=0.35+0.57-0
\\\\
&=0.92
\end{align}$

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{Oddems Family OR Thirteenth Night}\right)=0.92$

assessment_items.question_content:
A civics teacher asked her students to indicate whether they believed each of two headlines. One headline was false and the other was true, but the students did not know this. The probability that a student selected at random believed the true headline was $90\%$ and the probability that the student believed the false headline was $82\%$. She found that $75\%$ of the students believed both headlines.

**In this sample, are the events "believed the false headline" and "believed the true headline" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected person from this sample believed the true headline OR believed the false headline.**

$P\left(\text{believed true OR believed false}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

There are people in this sample who believed both the true and the false headlines.

So these events are not mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a person who believed the true headline OR a person who believed the false headline

$\begin{align}&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{believed OR believed false}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{believed true}\right)+P\left(\text{believed false}\right)-P\left(\text{both}\right)
\\\\
&=0.9+0.82-0.75
\\\\
&=0.97
\end{align}$

##The answer

No, the events are not mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{believed true OR believed false}\right)=0.97$

assessment_items.question_content:
When registering their cars, owners must identify the color that covers the largest portion of the vehicle. In one region, the probability that car was mainly white was $0.24$, the probability that it was mainly silver was $0.22$, and the probability that it was mainly black was $0.19$.

**In this region, are the events "silver" and "black" mutually exclusive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Find the probability that a randomly selected car from this region is mainly silver OR mainly black.**

$P\left(\text{silver OR black}\right)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
##Are the events mutually exclusive?

Mutually exclusive events cannot happen at the same time.

The owner has to identify a single color that covers the largest portion of the car, so the owner cannot identify a single car as both silver and black.

So these events are mutually exclusive.

##Calculating the probability of getting a car that is mainly silver OR mainly black

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}P\left(\text{silver OR black}\right)
\\\\
&=P\left(\text{silver}\right)+P\left(\text{black}\right)-P\left(\text{both}\right)
\\\\
&=0.22+0.19-0
\\\\
&=0.41
\end{align}$

##The answer

Yes, the events are mutually exclusive.

$P\left(\text{silver OR black}\right)=0.41$
